title: Brassica
alt: Sequoia Brussels sprouts are delicious but it's pretty hard to finish one.
transcript:
[Cueball, Megan and another Cueball are standing in front of a large tree.  Only the lowest section of the tree trunk is visible.]
 Cueball: Did you know the mighty redwood is actually the same species as broccoli and kale? It's just a different cultivar.
 Other Cueball: Wow!

 [Caption below the panel]
 Every year or two, botanists add another plant to Brassica oleracea and see if anyone calls them on it.
explanation:
is a plant species, that contains 23 different cultivars (plants which evolved primarily due to human selection) of wild cabbage, a relatively nondescript herb, to which many vegetables that we eat belong. These vegetables look quite different from each other, though all share the same basic appearance; compare, for example, , ,  and .

In the comic, Cueball, who is serving as a natural-history tour guide or park ranger, or maybe is just leading a group of friends, declares that the "mighty redwood" (presumably the coast redwood,  ) also belongs to this species. Since the coast redwood is a , while B. oleracea is a , the two species are about as different as two land plants can be, both in classification and appearance.

However, when viewed from high above, the canopy of forests can bear a striking resemblance to the top of a head of many of these cultivars. In this case, the pointier tops of conifers would more likely resemble a , while broad-leaved forests would be closer to the more commonly encountered calabrese. Such far-fetched resemblances could be used by a botanist as a joke to see if anyone is paying enough attention to call them out, which according to the caption, seems like something botanists do every year or two.

The title text refers to Sequoia Brussels sprouts. The reference is probably to the Giant Sequoia (), a close relative of the coast redwood. "Resinous" is probably a more apt adjective than "delicious", and they're probably woody. Additionally, as stated, they would indeed be quite hard to finish - Sequoia trees can range from  in height, and so consuming them will take weeks or maybe months, a monotonous task despite their "deliciousness". It is probably no more advisable to stand under a sequoia bearing sprouts than it is to stand under a cannonball tree.
